perturbation.original_text: An entity controls another entity when it owns or controls, directly or indirectly, fifty-one percent (51%) or more of the equity share capital issued and outstanding of the other entity or when it controls, on its own or jointly, the majority of the composition of the Board of Directors of such other entity.
perturbation.changed_text: An entity controls another entity when it owns or controls, directly or indirectly, twenty-five percent (25%) or more of the equity share capital issued and outstanding of the other entity or when it controls, on its own, a minority of the composition of the Board of Directors of such other entity.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of 'control' aligns with standard corporate law practices. Reducing the equity ownership threshold to 25% and changing 'majority' control of the board to 'minority' control fundamentally alters the definition. This creates a conflict because typical legal and business definitions of control require a higher threshold of ownership or board representation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Exchange Act of 1934 - Definition of Control
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts the SEC's and general understanding of what constitutes 'control' as it pertains to securities and corporate governance. According to 17 CFR § 230.405, control means the possession, direct or indirect, of the power to direct or cause the direction of the management and policies of a person, whether through the ownership of voting securities, by contract, or otherwise. The modification lowers the equity threshold to 25% and speaks of minority board control, which is not in line with how regulators or courts would view the ability to 'direct' a company's management and policies.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 17 CFR § 230.405 - Definitions of terms. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 230.405 - Definitions of terms. CFR Table of Popular Names prev | next § 230.405 Definitions of terms. Unless the context otherwise requires, all terms used in §§ 230.400 to 230.494, inclusive, or in the forms for registration have the same meanings as in the Act and in the general rules and regulations . In addition, the following definitions apply, unless the context otherwise requires: Affiliate. An affiliate of, or person affiliated with, a specified person, is a person that directly, or indirectly through one or more intermediaries, controls or is controlled by, or is under common control with, the person specified. Amount. The term amount, when used in regard to securities, means the principal amount if relating to evidences of indebtedness, the number of shares if relating to shares , and the number of units if relating to any other kind of security . Associate. The term associate, when used to indicate a relationship with any person, means (1) a corporation or organization (other than the registrant or a majority-owned subsidiary of the registrant) of which such person is an officer or partner or is, directly or indirectly, the beneficial owner of 10 percent or more of any class of equity securities, (2) any trust or other estate in which such person has a substantial beneficial interest or as to which such person serves as trustee or in a similar capacity, and (3) any relative or spouse of such person, or any relative of such spouse, who has the same home as such person or who is a director or officer of the registrant or any of its parents or subsidiaries. Automatic shelf registration statement. The term automatic shelf registration statement means a registration statement filed on Form S-3, Form F-3, or Form N-2 ( § 239.13 , § 239.33 , or §§ 239.14 and 274.11a-1 of this chapter) by
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the typical understanding and legal definition of 'control' by lowering the equity ownership threshold to 25% and referring to minority control of the board. The original contract aligns with standard corporate law practices by requiring 51% or more of equity share capital. According to 17 CFR § 230.405, control means the possession, direct or indirect, of the power to direct or cause the direction of the management and policies of a person, whether through the ownership of voting securities, by contract, or otherwise. Lowering the equity threshold to 25% does not align with regulators or courts views on the ability to direct a company's management and policies. Therefore, the change results in non-compliance with the law, making the contract unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: All matters, questions, disputes, differences or claims arising between the Parties as to the effect, interpretation or application of this Agreement or as to their rights, duties or liabilities hereunder, or as to any act, matter or thing arising out of consequent to, or in connection with this Agreement (hereinafter called the Difference') will be resolved amicably through negotiations.
perturbation.changed_text: All matters, questions, disputes, differences or claims arising between the Parties as to the effect, interpretation or application of this Agreement or as to their rights, duties or liabilities hereunder, or as to any act, matter or thing arising out of consequent to, or in connection with this Agreement (hereinafter called the Difference') will be resolved through physical combat. Each party will select a representative to engage in a duel to resolve the dispute.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing amicable negotiations with 'physical combat' directly contradicts the purpose and enforceability of arbitration clauses. It introduces an absurd and legally unacceptable method of dispute resolution.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification directly contradicts the FAA, which validates arbitration agreements for commercial disputes. The FAA requires the dispute resolution to be settled via means of mutual agreement, not violence.

Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate A written provision in any maritime transaction or a contract evidencing a transaction involving commerce to settle by arbitration a controversy thereafter arising out of such contract or transaction, or the refusal to perform the whole or any part thereof, or an agreement in writing to submit to arbitration an existing controversy arising ou
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces 'amicable negotiations' with 'physical combat' for dispute resolution, which directly contradicts the FAA's validation of arbitration agreements for commercial disputes, which is supported by snippet 1 stating the validity and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate. This change is a clear departure from legally accepted methods of dispute resolution and would likely render the agreement unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: IMPCO expressly agrees that during the existence of this Agreement, IMPCO shall not enter into any other Joint Venture Agreement or Marketing/Distribution Agreement, with any company or person(s) in the Territory with respect to the Products.
perturbation.changed_text: IMPCO expressly agrees that during the existence of this Agreement, IMPCO may enter into any other Joint Venture Agreement or Marketing/Distribution Agreement, with any company or person(s) in the Territory with respect to the Products, if they so choose.
perturbation.explanation: This modifies the **non-compete clause** such that it explicitly allows IMPCO to engage in activities that directly compete with the joint venture. This undermines the fundamental purpose of the clause.
perturbation.law_explanation: While facially, this clause might not obviously contradict antitrust law, it removes a reasonable restriction on competition that could be seen as beneficial to the joint venture and to the Indian market. By allowing IMPCO to compete freely, it creates the potential for IMPCO to dominate the market or undermine the JVC. This introduces complexities regarding fair competition and market dominance. Even though it seems that the agreement is creating more competition by letting IMPCO create more joint ventures, it's possible for one corporation to squeeze another out of the market.

The tables below are for the entire Public Law. Pub. L. Section Status USC Title USC Section Authorizes 1 15 1 2 15 2 3 15 3 4 15 4 5 15 5 6 15 6 7 15 6a 8 15 7 TOPN: Table of Popular Names Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts the original intent of the non-compete clause by allowing IMPCO to engage in competing ventures, which undermines the purpose of the joint venture and potentially violates antitrust principles. The original contract restricted IMPCO from entering into competing agreements, whereas the changed contract explicitly permits such actions. The LLM explanation suggests this creates potential for IMPCO to dominate the market, and while Scraped Law Snippet 2 is empty, Scraped Law Snippet 1 references the Sherman Act which aims to prevent monopolies and restraint of trade. While the snippet is minimal, the removal of the restriction on competition raises concerns under antitrust law by allowing IMPCO to potentially undermine the joint venture.
